source.manual: AASHTO Design of Pavement Structures (1993), PCA Thickness Design for Concrete Highway and Street Pavements, General Practice

description: Universal rigid pavement types with characteristics, applicability, and design parameters

pavement_types.description: Unreinforced concrete slabs with closely-spaced transverse contraction joints. The most common rigid pavement type worldwide.
pavement_types.transverse_joints: Contraction joints at 4-6 m spacing, dowelled for traffic > 5 MCESA
pavement_types.longitudinal_joints: Tie bars at lane and shoulder interfaces
advantages: Simple construction -- no reinforcement to place
Well-understood design methods (AASHTO 1993, PCA)
Lower material cost than reinforced types
Widest range of traffic applicability
limitations: Many joints to construct and maintain
Joint performance critical to pavement life
Joint faulting and pumping possible without dowels

pavement_types.description: Concrete slabs with distributed reinforcement allowing wider joint spacing. Reinforcement holds mid-panel cracks tight, not for structural capacity.
pavement_types.reinforcement: Distributed mesh or bar reinforcement, 0.10-0.25% of cross-section
pavement_types.transverse_joints: Contraction joints at 8-15 m spacing, dowelled
pavement_types.longitudinal_joints: Tie bars at lane and shoulder interfaces
advantages: Fewer joints than JPCP -- reduced construction and maintenance cost
Cracks held tight by reinforcement -- maintains aggregate interlock
Longer slab panels reduce joint-related distresses
limitations: Higher material cost (reinforcement)
More complex construction (steel placement)
Steel corrosion risk if cracks exceed 0.5 mm width

pavement_types.description: No transverse joints. Continuous longitudinal reinforcement (0.60-0.70%) controls natural cracking to produce tight, closely-spaced transverse cracks.
pavement_types.longitudinal_joints: Tie bars at lane and shoulder interfaces
advantages: No transverse joints -- excellent ride quality
Low maintenance -- no joint sealing or faulting
Longest service life of rigid pavement types (40+ years)
Best suited for very heavy traffic corridors
limitations: Highest initial cost (continuous reinforcement)
Complex terminal treatments at ends and structures
Difficult to repair -- punchouts require full-depth patching
Not economical for low-traffic roads

pavement_types.description: Similar to CRCP but used as a base layer with thick asphalt surfacing (>=180 mm). Combines concrete rigidity with asphalt surface characteristics.
pavement_types.longitudinal_joints: Tie bars at lane and shoulder interfaces
advantages: Surface maintenance by milling and overlay (no concrete surface repair)
Excellent structural capacity from concrete base
Good ride quality from asphalt surface
Can accommodate surface rutting repair without structural impact
limitations: Highest overall thickness (concrete base + asphalt surface)
Two construction operations (concrete then asphalt)
Reflection cracking through asphalt possible
Highest initial cost of all pavement types

pavement_types.description: Zero-slump concrete placed with asphalt paving equipment and compacted with vibratory rollers. No dowels, no forms. Rapid, economical placement.
pavement_types.transverse_joints: Contraction joints by early sawcutting at 3-6 m spacing
advantages: Very rapid construction (no curing forms, no dowels)
Lower cost than conventional concrete
Uses asphalt paving equipment (widely available)
Suitable for large areas (industrial yards, ports)
limitations: Rough surface texture -- may need asphalt overlay for ride comfort
Lower flexural strength than conventional concrete
No dowel load transfer -- relies on aggregate interlock
Limited to lower speeds without asphalt surface

pavement_types.description: Interlocking concrete blocks (60-100 mm thick) laid on sand bedding over structural base. Labour-intensive, individually replaceable blocks.
pavement_types.transverse_joints: None (continuous surface with sand-filled joints between blocks)
pavement_types.longitudinal_joints: Edge restraint required (kerbing or concrete edge beam)
advantages: Labour-intensive construction (employment generation)
Individual blocks replaceable (easy maintenance)
No curing period (immediate trafficking after laying)
Can be constructed with minimal equipment
Suitable for irregular shapes and areas around structures
limitations: Limited to low/medium traffic and low speeds
Block displacement possible under heavy braking/turning
Sand bedding susceptible to washing out if drainage is poor
Ride quality inferior to concrete or asphalt

pavement_types.description: Natural or dressed stone blocks (100-200 mm thick) laid on sand or mortar bedding. Very durable, locally-sourced material, labour-intensive construction.
pavement_types.transverse_joints: None (continuous surface with mortar or sand-filled joints)
advantages: Very long service life (50+ years)
Uses locally available natural stone
Labour-intensive (employment-intensive programmes)
Extremely durable in tropical climates
Low technology requirement
limitations: Rough ride quality -- noise and vibration
Very low speed suitability only
Difficult access to underground services
Skilled stonemasons required for dressed stone
Not suitable for high-speed or heavy traffic
